question: An 18-year-old patient with severe abdominal pain, marked abdominal distension, and peritoneal signs underwent emergent abdominal CT on admission. There is clinical concern for a major intra-abdominal injury with hemodynamic compromise. Based on the clinical presentation and imaging, which of the following is contraindicated at this time?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the axial contrast-enhanced CT image for abnormalities that explain the clinical scenario of severe abdominal pain, distension, and peritoneal signs. I see a large, heterogeneous fluid/soft-tissue collection in the upper abdomen that abuts and compresses adjacent structures (including the great vessels and bowel). Within and around this collection there are regions of higher attenuation that are concerning for recent or ongoing hemorrhage. I also note small pockets of extraluminal gas adjacent to the colon consistent with free intraperitoneal air, which supports bowel perforation and peritonitis.

Next, I relate these imaging findings to the question, which asks which intervention is contraindicated given suspected major intra‑abdominal injury with hemodynamic compromise. The key image-driven concerns are active intra‑abdominal bleeding and a likely bowel perforation with peritonitis.

Then I consider how each option aligns with those observations. Systemic anticoagulation (IV heparin) would increase bleeding risk and could worsen an ongoing hemorrhage in the large intra‑abdominal collection and any vascular injury — therefore it is contraindicated. Emergency exploratory laparotomy is appropriate for a patient with peritoneal signs, free air, and possible uncontrolled hemorrhage and thus is not contraindicated. Transarterial embolization can be a means to control arterial bleeding when localized and is not intrinsically contraindicated by these findings. Repeat CT angiography might provide further vascular detail but could be deferred if the patient is hemodynamically unstable; however, it is not universally contraindicated. Initiation of broad‑spectrum antibiotics is indicated for suspected bowel perforation and peritonitis.

In summary, given the CT evidence of a large hemorrhagic collection and possible ongoing bleeding plus signs of perforation, systemic anticoagulation (IV heparin) would be contraindicated at this time.
caption: Abdomen CT scan. Day of admission
sub_caption: Abdomen CT scan. Day of admission
image_context: An 18-year-old patient was admitted to the hospital due to severe abdominal pain. The medical interview revealed that the day before admission, the patient suffered blunt abdominal trauma during workout. Pain, which was located mainly in the epigastric region, was accompanied by nausea, vomiting and stoppage of bowel movements. The physical examination revealed severe flatulence coexisting with marked peritoneal signs. Pre-operative investigations showed WBC 19.3 × 103/µl (4.0–11.0) with increased total neutrophil count of 16.27 × 103 (2.20–4.80) and total bilirubin level 25.5 µmol/l (5.0–21.0). There were no other abnormalities. Because of the severe general condition of the patient, we decided to perform an abdominal CT scan, which revealed the presence of a non-homogeneous area of size 8.2 × 8.5 × 5 cm extending between the pancreas, transverse colon, the right lobe of the liver, aorta and vena cava. The lesion was initially described as hematoma, which caused oppression of adjacent organs. In addition, a description of the CT suggested the possibility of damage to the anterior wall of the inferior vena cava at a length of about 2 cm below the mouth of the renal vein. After administration of contrast, radiology test suggested that there were some features indicating the possibility of continuous bleeding. Moreover, near the descending colon there were some features indicating free air in the abdominal cavity, the CT image indicating the possibility of post-traumatic rupture of the intestine (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="WO-17-20413-g001">Fig. 1</xref>). Therefore there was no more time for additional examinations and the patient was qualified for urgent laparotomy. To our great surprise, intraoperatively we found an enormous, disintegrating retroperitoneal tumor. The tumor infiltrated the wall of the aorta and vena cava. We performed a cytoreductive procedure. The biological material was sent for pathological examination. On the first day after the operation we assessed serum tumor markers which revealed LDH (lactate dehydrogenase) 344 U/l (80–240 U/l), AFP (α-fetoprotein) 12 270 ng/ml (< 8 ng/ml). β-hCG (β-human chorionic gonadotropin) and CEA (carcinoembryonic antigen) were within normal limits. We did not detect any focal lesions in testicular ultrasound examination. The final pathological examination was established after one week (mixed germ cell tumor: embryonal carcinoma and yolk sac tumor).). We did not detect any focal lesions in testicular ultrasound examination. The final pathological examination was established after one week (mixed germ cell tumor: embryonal carcinoma and yolk sac tumor).
